name: Local HuggingFace Sentiment API

goal.answer: Set up a Flask service on port 5000 that performs sentiment analysis using the locally-cached distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english model from Hugging Face.

criteria.answer:
distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english model files exist at /app/model_cache/sentiment_model (config.json and weights present).
A Flask app exposes POST /sentiment on 0.0.0.0:5000 that accepts {"text": "..."} and returns {"sentiment": "positive"|"negative", "confidence": {"positive": float, "negative": float}}.
Invalid or missing input returns HTTP 400 with {"error": "..."}.
Service is running as a background process and accessible via curl from the host.
curl POST to http://localhost:5000/sentiment with a known positive sample returns sentiment=positive with confidence scores summing to ~1.0.

constraints.answer: Model must be downloaded and saved locally to /app/model_cache/sentiment_model (offline-capable thereafter)., Service must bind to 0.0.0.0:5000 and run as a background process., Python packages may be installed system-wide; Hugging Face transformers and Flask are acceptable dependencies., Response schema must exactly match the spec (sentiment string, confidence.positive, confidence.negative)., Bad requests must yield HTTP 400 with a JSON error body.

phases.answer: Download model and install dependencies, Build and launch Flask sentiment service

assumptions.answer: The target environment has internet access for the one-time model download, Python 3 with pip available, and sufficient disk and RAM for the ~250MB DistilBERT model. We assume the standard Hugging Face transformers sentiment-analysis pipeline (positive/negative labels) maps directly to the required response. The service is intended for local development use, not production hardening (no auth, no rate limiting, no HTTPS).

phases.name: Download model and install dependencies
phases.goal: Install transformers/torch/Flask system-wide and download distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english into /app/model_cache/sentiment_model.
phases.description: Install required Python packages and cache the sentiment model locally so the API can load it offline.

steps.description: Install transformers, torch, and Flask via pip.

verification.command: python3 -c 'import transformers, torch, flask; print(transformers.__version__, torch.__version__, flask.__version__)'

steps.description: Download distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english and save it to /app/model_cache/sentiment_model using snapshot_download or pipeline(...).

phases.summary: Phase 1 installed transformers/torch/flask system-wide and downloaded distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english to /app/model_cache/sentiment_model. Both steps verified by real imports and on-disk file existence.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

phases.name: Build and launch Flask sentiment service
phases.goal: Write the Flask app at /app/sentiment_service.py with a /sentiment POST endpoint, launch it on 0.0.0.0:5000 in the background, and verify it responds correctly.
phases.description: Implement the Flask API, start it as a background process, and verify behavior with curl.

steps.description: Write /app/sentiment_service.py: load the pipeline from /app/model_cache/sentiment_model, expose POST /sentiment that validates input, runs inference, and returns the documented JSON. Handle errors with HTTP 400.
Launch the service in the background with nohup, log to /app/sentiment_service.log, and confirm the process is listening on port 5000.

verification.command: ss -ltn | grep ':5000' && curl -fsS -X POST http://localhost:5000/sentiment -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"text":"I love this"}'

steps.description: Smoke-test additional cases: a clearly negative sentence returns negative, missing 'text' field returns 400, and confidence scores sum to ~1.0.

verification.command: curl -fsS -X POST http://localhost:5000/sentiment -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"text":"This is terrible"}' | python3 -c 'import json,sys; d=json.load(sys.stdin); assert d["sentiment"]=="negative"; s=d["confidence"]["positive"]+d["confidence"]["negative"]; assert 0.99<=s<=1.01' && curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' -X POST http://localhost:5000/sentiment -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{}' | grep -q '^400$'

phases.summary: Phase 2 wrote /app/sentiment_service.py (Flask app loading the local DistilBERT pipeline, validating input, returning documented JSON with 400 on bad input), launched it as a background process (PID 273) bound to 0.0.0.0:5000, and verified behavior with two live HTTP probes: positive ("I love this" → sentiment=positive), negative ("This is terrible" → sentiment=negative, sum=1.0), and missing text returns 400.

grade.rationale: All gates pass; project checks validate.

goal: Set up a Flask service on port 5000 that performs sentiment analysis using the locally-cached distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english model from Hugging Face.
successCriteria: distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english model files exist at /app/model_cache/sentiment_model (config.json and weights present).
A Flask app exposes POST /sentiment on 0.0.0.0:5000 that accepts {"text": "..."} and returns {"sentiment": "positive"|"negative", "confidence": {"positive": float, "negative": float}}.
Invalid or missing input returns HTTP 400 with {"error": "..."}.
Service is running as a background process and accessible via curl from the host.
curl POST to http://localhost:5000/sentiment with a known positive sample returns sentiment=positive with confidence scores summing to ~1.0.
constraints: Model must be downloaded and saved locally to /app/model_cache/sentiment_model (offline-capable thereafter).
Service must bind to 0.0.0.0:5000 and run as a background process.
Python packages may be installed system-wide; Hugging Face transformers and Flask are acceptable dependencies.
Response schema must exactly match the spec (sentiment string, confidence.positive, confidence.negative).
Bad requests must yield HTTP 400 with a JSON error body.

grade.rationale: Both phases delivered cleanly without retries. Phase 2's F1 used genuine end-to-end verification (live urllib POSTs with assertion script against the running Flask service on 0.0.0.0:5000), and the gate rationales cite specific concrete evidence — PID 273, 256MB safetensors weights, exact confidence values (~99.99%/~99.96%), specific package versions, and named files (config.json, model.safetensors, pytorch_model.bin, vocab.txt). The agent was appropriately honest that Phase 1's file-existence check was a proxy, offset by Phase 2's load-bearing end-to-end HTTP probes, and C1/C2/C3 each grounded in the specific success criteria rather than vague claims.
